text: The Vizera ELITE II endoscopic system is used to perform lymph node mapping and evaluate anastomosis with infrared fluorescent imaging during laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery.

This is a sixty-year-old male with rectal adenocarcinoma, six centimeters above an averge. The clinical stage was IIIb. We gave him conventional concurrent chemoradiotherapy. The clinical response was good with downsizing and downstaging, so we performed laparoscopic TME twelve weeks later.
The surgeon observes the emergence of fluorescence from the pelvis to the parodic area under infrared mode I, following the injection of ICG into the submucosal layer around the tumor.

The surgeon performs lymph node sampling at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), followed by the transection of the IMA and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The paralytic lymph node mapping is visible, but radical dissection is not performed due to negative sampling results.

The surgeon transects the rectum and removes the tumor. The colonic stump is retrieved through the umbilical wound. After injecting ICG into the peripheral vein of the left forearm, fluorescent enhancement is clearly visible over the colonic stump within thirty seconds.

The image system is switched to mode two to assess tissue perfusion. The surgeon performs intracorporeal anastomosis, followed by a final check of the anastomosis using ICG fluorescent imaging to ensure adequate perfusion.
The pathology was stage one with fourteen retrievable negative lymph nodes.
